old_claim.note: Stone Tool Making is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Stone tools are part of the simple toolkit used by early humans while gathering, hunting, scavenging, and processing food.

source_shape.source_locator: How They Survived section says prehistoric Homo sapiens made and used stone tools and that all humans gathered plants and hunted or scavenged animals for food.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source connects early human food procurement with stone-tool use by prehistoric Homo sapiens.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Stone_tool_making is not merely a distant predecessor here; the scoped node explicitly includes simple tool use for food procurement.

ontology_before: Basic Hunting & Gathering treated Stone Tool Making as only an earlier historical predecessor while also describing a simple toolkit.
ontology_after: Basic Hunting & Gathering treats Stone Tool Making as an enabling toolkit component for gathering, hunting, scavenging, and food processing.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: stone_tool_making->hunting_gathering_basic is now an enabling edge with direct official-agency evidence.
would_reject_if: The hunting_gathering_basic node were narrowed to tool-free food collection.
A stronger source showed the scoped Homo sapiens forager subsistence did not use stone tools.
The stone_tool_making node were narrowed to a later specialized tool industry unrelated to simple food procurement.
short_reason: Stone tools are an active component of the scoped simple forager toolkit.
